intro_band.agent_remit_summary: OpenHands is an autonomous software-engineering agent that accepts natural-language tasks and completes them end-to-end by writing and running code, browsing the web, and operating on connected Git/issue-tracker integrations, with all agent-produced code and file I/O confined to a per-conversation sandboxed runtime. This remit scopes two coupled components; the primary subject here is the app_server — the FastAPI control plane that authenticates users, provisions sandboxes, brokers secrets, stores conversation events, and mediates every integration call. Secrets must be encrypted at rest and returned masked; high-impact actions (cross-repo writes, PR merges, force-push, new integrations/MCP servers) require human approval; agent code must never execute on the host outside the sandbox; and every tool action must be written to a durable per-conversation event store.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python FastAPI application (openhands/app_server/, ~36k LOC) exposing a /api/v1 router (conversations, sandboxes, secrets, settings, git, webhooks) plus a mounted FastMCP server at /mcp; the legacy openhands/server/ is a deprecated shim re-exporting the app. The agentic core (openhands-sdk/-tools/-agent-server, pinned ==1.36.0) is a separate package and is out of this snapshot, so tool-execution, prompt-construction, and untrusted-content handling are not visible here. Sandboxes are provisioned via a Docker backend (non-privileged, host-network off by default) using the host Docker socket, or optionally as bare host subprocesses (ProcessSandboxService). Auth on the control plane is gated by get_dependencies(), which enforces nothing in the default OSS mode; secrets are persisted by FileSecretsStore, and JWT/JWE, session-key, and event-store subsystems are custom-built.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a well-engineered set of security primitives sitting behind a permissive default posture. The control plane ships genuinely strong controls — algorithm-pinned JWE, dual-authenticated secret delivery, running-only session keys, authenticated webhook ingress, a durable event store, and secret-redacting log filters — yet get_dependencies() enforces no authentication at all in the default self-hosted mode, and the shipped docker-compose.yml binds the API to every interface, so the entire /api/v1 surface (secrets writes, LLM-settings redirection, sandbox lifecycle) is reachable with no credential. Two independent weaknesses compound the exposure: provider tokens are persisted in plaintext by FileSecretsStore despite an unused encryption helper, and the mounted MCP PR/MR tools accept a model-supplied repo_name with no binding to the task-origin repository and no approval gate. The remaining findings are default-off safety valves — confirmation policy defaults to NeverConfirm, budget is uncapped by default, and an opt-in process runtime runs the agent on the host with the full environment.

rules.rule_text: Executing agent-generated code, or reading/writing files, on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.
Sharing state, memory, conversation events, or credentials across users or across conversations/sessions.
Auto-upgrading its own code, the pinned SDK packages, or its dependencies at runtime.
Running as an always-on background service that initiates tasks with no user request and no active conversation.
Any channel not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Any outbound destination, tool server, or integration not present in the operator's configuration.
User-configured secrets held in the secrets store (must be encrypted at rest and returned masked by default).
Raw secret values returned to, or routed through, the SDK client rather than delivered SaaS→sandbox under the required dual authentication (Bearer token and an active-sandbox session key).
API keys, tokens, or secret values written into logs, error messages, trajectories, or the model's context window.
Any conversation's state, memory, events, or credentials crossing into another conversation or another user.
Writes to any repository or organization other than the one the task originated in.
Any high-impact action (exec / send / delete outside the sandbox) when the deployment runs with confirmation mode enabled — the agent must pause for user confirmation before executing.
Running agent-generated code on the host OS outside the sandbox.
Leaking API keys or session tokens into logs, error messages, trajectories, or model context.
Every tool action is written to the durable per-conversation event store as it happens.
the agent iterates within the configured iteration/step cap (default order ~500 iterations) and per-task budget; on exceeding a cap it halts cleanly rather than retrying indefinitely.
Sandbox escape: any path by which agent-generated code or file I/O could reach the host (host-network sandbox, privileged containers, workspace mount escape, symlink traversal).
Budget, iteration, and age caps actually enforced (not merely declared) so a runaway conversation halts.
A secret value is detected in a location it should never occupy (log, error, trajectory, model context, or a Git diff).

the app server — the FastAPI backend in openhands/app_server/ (and the openhands-agent-server package) that authenticates users, provisions sandboxes, brokers secrets, stores events, and mediates every integration call.
The pinned OpenHands Software Agent SDK packages (openhands-sdk, openhands-agent-server, openhands-tools, all ==1.35.0).
Operator-registered; deliveries must be authenticated.

findings.summary: Default OSS deployment serves the entire V1 control plane with no authentication and binds it to all host interfaces.
findings.description: In the default self-hosted mode get_dependencies() returns an empty dependency list (no SESSION_API_KEY set, app_mode OPENHANDS), so every /api/v1 route — secrets writes, LLM-settings changes, and sandbox start/list/delete — is served with no credential, and the shipped docker-compose.yml maps port 3000 on every interface. A network-adjacent attacker can store git tokens, repoint the LLM base_url in settings to exfiltrate the server-held provider key and prompts, and drive the sandbox lifecycle. Enabling SESSION_API_KEY is the opt-in that closes this, but it is off by default.
policy_rule_text: the app server — the FastAPI backend in openhands/app_server/ (and the openhands-agent-server package) that authenticates users, provisions sandboxes, brokers secrets, stores events, and mediates every integration call.

evidence.snippet: get_dependencies() returns [] when SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode != SAAS — no auth dependency is attached to any v1 router (lines 23-32)
ports "3000:3000" publishes the app on all host interfaces; the compose file sets no SESSION_API_KEY, so the API is reachable unauthenticated

recommended_actions: Require an authentication dependency by default in OSS mode (fail-closed) and generate/persist a SESSION_API_KEY on first run rather than leaving the API open when the variable is unset.
In docker-compose.yml bind the published port to 127.0.0.1:3000:3000 and document that exposing it requires SESSION_API_KEY.

findings.summary: Provider tokens and custom secrets are persisted to disk in plaintext, despite an unused encryption helper.
findings.description: FileSecretsStore.store() serializes secrets with expose_secrets=True and writes the raw values to secrets.json in the persistence directory, so GitHub/GitLab PATs and user custom secrets sit in cleartext at rest. The remit requires secrets to be encrypted at rest; masking-on-read is honored (the API returns **********) but at-rest protection is absent. JwtService.encrypt_value() already exists and is unused by this store.
policy_rule_text: User-configured secrets held in the secrets store (must be encrypted at rest and returned masked by default).

evidence.snippet: store() calls secrets.model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True}) and writes it to secrets.json — raw token values persisted, no encryption
provider/custom serializers emit get_secret_value() when expose_secrets is set, so the persisted JSON holds cleartext tokens (lines 58-107)

recommended_actions: Encrypt secret values before persistence using the existing JwtService.encrypt_value() (or an equivalent envelope) and decrypt on load, so secrets.json never holds plaintext.

findings.summary: Mounted MCP PR/MR tools accept a model-supplied repository with no binding to the task-origin repo and no approval gate.
findings.description: The app-server's FastMCP tools (create_pr, create_mr, and the Bitbucket/Azure variants) take repo_name straight from model output and open a pull/merge request against it using the user's provider token, with no check that the target matches the task-origin repository and no confirmation step at this layer. An injected instruction can therefore redirect a write to any repository within the token's scope, which the remit designates an approval-gated action.
policy_rule_text: Writes to any repository or organization other than the one the task originated in.

evidence.snippet: create_pr(repo_name, source_branch, target_branch, ...) opens a PR on the model-provided repo_name with no origin-repo check or approval (create_mr/bitbucket/azure variants follow the same pattern)

recommended_actions: Bind PR/MR tools to the conversation's origin repository (resolved server-side from conversation context) and require explicit approval before writing to any other repository or organization.

findings.summary: CORS middleware reflects any origin with credentials allowed when no origins are configured.
findings.description: LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin() returns True for any origin (not just localhost) when permitted_cors_origins is empty — the default — while the middleware sets allow_credentials=True. A malicious web page the operator visits can then drive the credentialed API cross-origin from the browser, an exposure that stands even when the port is bound to localhost.

evidence.snippet: is_allowed_origin returns True for any origin when no allow_origins configured (only a warning is logged), with allow_credentials=True set in __init__ (lines 26-56)

recommended_actions: Default to an explicit localhost-only allowlist and never reflect arbitrary origins while allow_credentials=True; require OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS to be set for any non-local origin.

findings.summary: Confirmation policy defaults to NeverConfirm, so high-impact actions run without human approval unless explicitly enabled.
findings.description: _select_confirmation_policy() returns NeverConfirm() whenever confirmation_mode is false, and the shipped config defaults confirmation_mode=false for headless/CLI. The approval machinery exists (ConfirmRisky/AlwaysConfirm when enabled) but is off by default, so the remit's require-confirmation intent for high-impact actions is not enforced out of the box; the actual per-action risk classification lives in the extracted SDK analyzer.
policy_rule_text: Any high-impact action (exec / send / delete outside the sandbox) when the deployment runs with confirmation mode enabled — the agent must pause for user confirmation before executing.

evidence.snippet: _select_confirmation_policy returns NeverConfirm() when confirmation_mode is False (lines 677-688)
confirmation_mode defaults to false (commented default) for headless/CLI deployments

recommended_actions: Default confirmation_mode to on (or fail-closed to AlwaysConfirm) for high-impact actions, and require an explicit operator opt-out rather than opt-in.

findings.summary: The process/local sandbox runtime spawns the agent server on the host inheriting the full environment, with no isolation.
findings.description: ProcessSandboxService._start_agent_process() starts the agent server via subprocess.Popen with env=os.environ.copy() and only a working-directory change — no filesystem, network, or capability isolation — so under RUNTIME=local/process the "sandbox" is a bare host process that inherits every host environment secret. The default Docker runtime does confine the agent (non-privileged, host-network off), so this is an opt-in mode that nonetheless breaches the remit's host-isolation boundary and leaks host credentials into the agent's environment.
policy_rule_text: Executing agent-generated code, or reading/writing files, on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.
Running agent-generated code on the host OS outside the sandbox.
Sandbox escape: any path by which agent-generated code or file I/O could reach the host (host-network sandbox, privileged containers, workspace mount escape, symlink traversal).

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy(); subprocess.Popen(cmd, env=env, cwd=working_dir, ...) — agent server runs on host with full inherited env, no isolation (lines 125-148)

recommended_actions: Scope the process-runtime environment to an explicit allowlist instead of copying the full host env, and gate the local/process runtime behind an explicit "unsafe, no isolation" operator acknowledgement.

findings.summary: The app-server container mounts the host Docker socket, giving the app process root-equivalent host control.
findings.description: docker-compose.yml bind-mounts /var/run/docker.sock into the app container so DockerSandboxService can provision sandboxes. This is functionally required for the Docker runtime, but it grants the app tier full control of the host Docker daemon; combined with the unauthenticated control plane (PRAX-...-001), a compromise of the app process escalates to host takeover via arbitrary container creation.

evidence.snippet: volumes mounts /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock into the app container (lines 18-19)

recommended_actions: Isolate Docker access behind a least-privilege socket proxy (e.g. tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy) restricted to the container operations the sandbox service needs, rather than mounting the raw socket.

findings.summary: The Tavily MCP proxy embeds the API key in the request URL query string.
findings.description: init_tavily_proxy() constructs the upstream client URL as https://mcp.tavily.com/mcp/?tavilyApiKey={key}, placing the secret in the query string where it is prone to appearing in proxy logs, browser history, and referrer headers. The app's own uvicorn access logs redact URL params via RedactURLParamsFilter, but the key still transits in the URL to the upstream and any intermediary.

evidence.snippet: StreamableHttpTransport(url=f'https://mcp.tavily.com/mcp/?tavilyApiKey={tavily_api_key}') — API key passed as a URL query parameter (lines 64-68)

recommended_actions: Pass the Tavily API key via an Authorization header or transport auth mechanism instead of the URL query string.

findings.summary: No per-task cost ceiling by default — the budget cap ships unlimited.
findings.description: The shipped config defaults max_budget_per_task=0.0, which means no spend limit, so a runaway or abused conversation has no monetary halt condition. Iteration (500), conversation age (~10 days), and per-user concurrency (3) caps are set, but without a budget ceiling the remit's requirement that caps actually halt a runaway conversation is only partly met; the halt enforcement itself lives in the extracted SDK.
policy_rule_text: the agent iterates within the configured iteration/step cap (default order ~500 iterations) and per-task budget; on exceeding a cap it halts cleanly rather than retrying indefinitely.
Budget, iteration, and age caps actually enforced (not merely declared) so a runaway conversation halts.

evidence.snippet: max_budget_per_task = 0.0 means no limit (default), while max_iterations = 500 is the only spend-adjacent cap set (lines 52-56)

recommended_actions: Ship a non-zero default max_budget_per_task (or require the operator to set one at startup) so every conversation has an enforced monetary ceiling.

positives.title: Algorithm-pinned JWE encryption
positives.description: JwtService restricts the JWE registry to dir + A256GCM and JWS to HS256 with key-id rotation, explicitly to prevent cryptographic-agility / algorithm-confusion attacks.

positives.title: Dual-authenticated, running-only secret delivery
positives.description: The /webhooks/secrets path requires a signed JWS access token, and session API keys are accepted only while the sandbox is RUNNING and are ownership-checked, limiting the blast radius of a leaked key.

positives.title: Authenticated webhook ingress
positives.description: All /webhooks/* callbacks from sandboxes are gated by an X-Session-API-Key validated against a live sandbox before events are persisted.

positives.title: Secret-redacting structured logging
positives.description: Logging installs a SensitiveDataFilter and RedactURLParamsFilter plus SDK redaction utilities and disables LiteLLM loggers, preventing keys and session tokens from reaching logs.

positives.title: Exact dependency pinning with committed lockfiles
positives.description: The agent SDK packages, litellm, and openai are exact-pinned and both poetry.lock and uv.lock are committed, giving reproducible, version-swap-resistant builds.

positives.title: Durable per-conversation event store
positives.description: Every agent tool action is persisted to a durable event store (filesystem/S3/GCS backends) via the authenticated event webhook, supporting incident reconstruction.

rows.purpose: durable action-level audit trail of every agent tool invocation and outcome

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. OpenHands' control plane is a mature codebase with real, operative security machinery — pinned cryptography, per-request session-key and dual-token authentication on the sandbox/webhook paths, exact dependency pinning with committed lockfiles, and a durable, secret-redacted event/audit trail — which earns Established marks in Supply Chain and Monitor. It is pulled down by Zero Trust: the default self-hosted deployment enforces no authentication on the API and ships it network-reachable, persists provider tokens in plaintext, and leaves approval gating and budget caps off by default. Much of the agent's runtime enforcement (untrusted-content trust boundaries, tool-call gating, cap-driven halting) lives in the extracted SDK and cannot be verified from this snapshot, so several strong clauses resolve as enforcement-not-possible.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The app server structurally scopes work to per-conversation sandboxes built from fixed sandbox specs with a concurrency cap, but the mounted MCP PR/MR tools accept an arbitrary repo_name with no binding to the task-origin repository, and the agent's own tool/domain restriction lives in the extracted SDK.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Secrets are returned masked by default and the event store is user-scoped, but FileSecretsStore persists provider tokens in plaintext at rest, and untrusted-content grounding/validation (web, microagents, repo files) is handled in the extracted agentic core and not visible here.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Strong primitives exist and run — algorithm-pinned JWE, running-only session keys, dual-token secret delivery, ownership checks — but the default OSS control plane enforces no authentication (get_dependencies() returns an empty list), CORS reflects any origin with credentials when unconfigured, and both the confirmation gate and budget cap default off.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The SDK packages, litellm, and openai are exact-pinned (==) with both poetry.lock and uv.lock committed and no hardcoded credentials in scope; the only softness is a floor range on fastmcp, covered by the lockfile.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Security-aware design is visible in code comments (algorithm pinning to prevent cryptographic-agility attacks, running-only session keys, redaction filters), but no adversarial-testing artifacts, injection-test suites, or evidence that red-team findings drove architecture appear in the scanned scope.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: A durable per-conversation event store records every agent action via authenticated webhooks, and structured (optional JSON) logging ships with secret-redaction filters (SensitiveDataFilter, RedactURLParamsFilter) and LiteLLM key-leak suppression; the gap is that file logging is off unless LOG_TO_FILE/DEBUG is set.
